comments.title: Divination
comments.content:
Hi JMG, thanks as always for this.

What’s been your go-to divination method lately? Do you alternate between things like tarot, geomancy etc - or is there one that you prefer over the others and why?

Thank you

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content: It depends on the subject. For quick accurate divinations about practical issues, geomancy has been my go-to for a couple of decades now -- nothing equals it.  If it has to do with spiritual or magical issues I typically use my Sacred Geometry Oracle, though I sometimes use Ogham or Coelbren for things relevant to Druidry. It's been years since I've used tarot -- I'll probably cycle back into that one of these days, though.

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content:
Hi John, not the OP but, out of curiosity, when you use geomancy do you normally use shield or house charts?

Thanks!

Manuel

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content: House charts. I find them extremely informative and accurate.

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content: I would agree, though I admit I don't use this much nowadays since I find them too close to Horary so I tend to default to the later... But I have to admit I miss those 16 figures!

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content:
I have taken to using house charts most of the time when I have a practical question that needs a quick answer. Nothing compares to it that I've found with regard to accuracy and detail. What an amazing tradition that we almost lost here in the West!

I still love the Rider-Waite deck and use it for daily divinations as well, and I have kept up said practice for years at this point. It is accurate too, in its own way, but it requires a bit more imagination and sometimes I have misread what it was telling me; e.g., I wondered if a situation was going the way I thought it was and I got 8 of Wands, which seemed to be saying yes - but in fact it was saying "the situation is changing quickly - act fast!". That is, confirmation bias can be a trouble for a sometimes-knucklehead like myself ;-).

I also have used geomancy for getting an idea about the character of some people I know and it's very useful for that. For my own self I got Rubeus, and even the physical description is spot on, along with the character. Of course, the descriptions in the book are only blurbs - I was much wilder in my youth. With age the passion of Rubeus can become intensity of practice and commitment, but it can be a pretty wild ride when undisciplined.

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content:
That difference in accuracies may be down, not to the technique itself, but to something about the populations that its egregor of practice is currently or has historically been confined to. Nowadays people practicing geomantic divination, on average, take it a lot more seriously and are so to speak better-"catechized" than people practicing more well-known techniques. I don't know how exactly such a difference would affect whatever the kind of attention it is that comes together around events where someone does a divination and then interprets it and maybe makes some decision and then retrospects on the outcome, but it would be surprising if there weren't some such effect. If the places of geomancy in history and in popular consciousness had been switched with Tarot or western astrology, then it might have now compared less favorably, such that it would instead be the other tradition whose loss you'd have been thinking of as a historical near-miss.

I kind of wish the name that had stuck had been something other than "geomancy", because just looking at the name you'd think it would be something more like electional feng shui (even if historically that meaning came later) or a scrying technique involving caves. Like maybe it should have been a name that had more of an etymological connection to divots drawn in sand somehow.

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content:
fortuitous question - i find myself drawn to house charts, but being a novice at geomancy had been trying to focus on the shield chart (under the impression that it is more traditional).

separate question: recently i was attempting a house chart to supplement standard astrology to answer "when in the next month would an action be most fortuitously undertaken?" but became somewhat confused. is this an unhelpful use of geomancy, or just beyond my experience at present?

replies.title: Re: Divination
replies.content:
1) Both charts are entirely traditional, being well documented in medieval Arabic and Latin sources.

2) Time is something that geomantic readings are not very good at.

comments.title: CGD Druid Grade Karmic Culmination
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I recently finished my 9 workings of the CGD Druid grade initiation after nearly 10 years of on again off again study in which I was maybe a C student at best, but I did do as much of the work as I felt I could get done, did my examinations, and finally did the initiations and a few more for good measure as divination prompted.

Aaaand that's landed me in a white hot furnace karmic culmination that has basically taken me to the edge of my sanity. I'm hauling myself back and getting plenty of sleep, and am being reminded every day with raps on the knuckles to check my runaway intuition with an outer planes check before I do anything silly.

However, I had no idea how incredibly serious this initiation was going to be. I didn't lose my job when taking the first grade. I feel like I may lose my life or at least my whole connection to everything I was doing beforehand before all this is over. A long time ago on MM, you described the black candle working to Saturn for clearing a lot of karma in a jiffy. Well this initiation's effects feel very much like that ritual you described. My oath was just to live in the knowledge we're all traveling the Old Straight Track up through the veil into the luminous life. I thought that was reasonably mundane and manageable. I was very wrong

I'm finding the Guardians of the Druidic temple were around me the whole time, tapping their foot and waiting impatiently for me to Get A Clue. And I am now getting a clue-by-four pallet of bricks dropped straight on my head.

Is the Druid grade culmination always this rough? Do you have any advice to navigate it?

Is the order associated with the book still in existence and would they be ones to look to for help or is this more something I need to work out on my own?

replies.title: Re: CGD Druid Grade Karmic Culmination
replies.content:
1) It really varies. Some people have more of a culmination with the Ovate or Bardic grades, and the intensity also varies depending on what kind of karma you've got built up.

2) The Druidical Order of the Golden Dawn is still very much in existence; I stepped down as its titular head once it had a good core group established. You'll find them at http://druidical-gd.org/ . I'd definitely recommend contacting them for help with this.

replies.title: Re: CGD Druid Grade Karmic Culmination
replies.content:
I haven't finished the Druid Grade, but I passed through the Ovate without issue then got wholloped right after the Bardic Grade initiation, so please accept my sympathies. I'm still working my way out of it and don't figure to be done any time soon, so you are not alone.

And as JMG said, yes, we, the DOGD, are still around. Feel free to stop by.

replies.title: Re: CGD Druid Grade Karmic Culmination
replies.content: You have my sympathy - or rather, my empathy: for I’ve been undergoing something similar. After ten years and  more of the Middle Pillar and related exercises, I’ve been getting karmically clobbered. You are not alone.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've been going through your Spirit and the Sword with an eye towards incorporating the practices into my self-rolled spiritual development/self care routine. I've been practicing the stretching outlined in the book and looking into getting a sword for the practices. So far, what I've come across that seems like it might work are the copies made of the 1840 and 1860 US cavalry sabers or something more along the lines of a mortuary sword (at least in terms of things I can afford that are still "functional"). I was curious though, what exactly was the sword you use(d)? And do you happen to have any updated advice after 15 years or so?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I used a replica of the US Army model 1840 noncommissioned officer's sword, which is still available from Atlanta Cutlery. As for updated advice, none really -- I injured my shoulder a couple of decades ago and it acts up when I do the exercises (I've tried at intervals, as they're otherwise very good for me) so that project has had to go by the boards, at least for me.

comments.title: Visualization and also protective Talismans
comments.content:
Good evening!

Two questions I'd like to ask you:
1. As I practice visualization (across all senses) one thing I am uncertain about is just how far is it reasonable to expect the development of the faculty. Particularly with sight-visualization, I have a very good ability to picture things in my mind, in detail, rotate them, etc. but they never escape my mind's eye. In other words, I can't imagine something so well that I actually "see" it through my eyes as opposed to what's "really" there in front of me. Is it possible to develop visualization such that one can then passively see things from the imagination in real space or must such images always remain in the mind's eye?

My inclination is to believe that what is visualized in the imagination stays in the mind's eye but a passage in Initiation into Hermetics discusses seeing something you project from the imagination as a fata morgana that appears to the sight in the place of what the physical eyes see. Which is not an ability I have developed, and wonder if I can.

2. There is someone in my life who has been a very negative influence, a family member, not just for me but for the rest of the family, and sometime ago I fashioned a talisman to protect me from her ill will and machinations. It has worked - unfortunately I believe that malice that had been sent my way is now targeting someone else. I don't want to get into details but the change was sudden and it defies logic why she would target her instead of me. I did not anticipate this. Can you give me some advice with this situation re: protection for the others?

replies.title: Re: Visualization and also protective Talismans
replies.content:
1) The imagery doesn't have to "escape the mind's eye." It works just as effectively as mental imagery. Some people have the capacity to project such images into the visual field, but most don't; I don't, for example. The lack of that ability has never slowed me down.

2) If you can get the other person's permission, you can create a talisman to protect her. You do need consent, though.

comments.title: The Emotional Grounding Effect
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I'm autistic and I was just pondering reasons for some interactions I've had with certain neurotypicals. I don't know if you share this experience but if you do it may deepen your understanding.

Many neurotypicals find autistics to be off-putting, but I and some others I talked to have found that a certain variety of neurotypical is drawn to us. This tends to be an extremely sociable, gregarious person who is always the life of the party. The reason for the attraction, as far as I can determine is this: these people tend to pick up high amounts of emotional "static" from others and we act as a kind of grounding wire to drain it away. These are often the kinds of people who others will confide in and want to tell their troubles to, and this adds to the load of emotional energy they're carrying. Autistics do not add to this load but instead can dissipate it by our mere presence.

Do you have any thoughts on the energetic factors at play here? I think it may be related to the similar dynamic between autistics and animals, which are often more comfortable around us than normal people. Thanks as always for these posts.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content: That's really quite remarkable. No, I'm not familiar with the effect and don't know how it functions on an energetic level. Have you considered developing clairvoyant abilities to try to work it out for yourself?

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
If I may piggyback on this (and if it's not too far off topic for a MM), there is a question I'd been wanting to ask: Do you have any tips for navigating intimate relationships (i.e. marriage) as a asperger's or asperger's-adjacent person?

My wife is also a very sensitive person who easily forms emotional connections with people. For me, I don't think I even understand what "emotional connection" means (in the way that a blind person wouldn't understand colors). It's just not something I have ever experienced. Twenty years on, we realize we are both a bit baffled about how we ended up together in the first place, and are trying to figure out how to relate to each other.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
I'm sorry to say I'm the wrong person to ask about that. My late wife was also autistic -- probably high-functioning autist rather than Aspergers -- and that was one of the great strengths of our relationship. We both found it very easy and comfortable to get along with the other's quirks and oddities, and it was an immense comfort to both of us that neither of us expected the other to pick up on what the other was thinking or feeling by osmosis or something -- when there was an issue, we sat down and talked about it, and took what the other was saying at face value rather than trying to read some arbitrary "real meaning" into it, as neurotypical people so often do.

I have no idea how I'd handle an intimate relationship with a neurotypical person, much less somebody with unusually high levels of empathy. Being a geek, I always dated other geeks, and it worked out quite well!

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
If you were single, the easy answer would be: find another aspie to marry ;)

No idea how to make it work with normies, other than the same things that work for pretty much all relationships: talk to each other, be honest, and if you're feeling irritated, don't respond immediately (and have a deal with each other that an immediate response is not required: it's ok to say 'I need to think about that first'), try to respect your partner even if you don't agree with them, and figure out your shared values and goals so you have something to work toward together.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
Thanks to both of you for your replies.
I like the "let me think about that" idea. Come to think of it, I might also add "what do you mean by that" to my repertoire.

It's interesting, though. I have come to understand that different people sense different things. I, for example, am good at giving massages because I can feel the ether (ki/prana/etc.). So I can easily find the right place to push on, where the blockages are, and where the sore spots are, just by running my hands along someone's body.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content: What sort of clairvoyance do you think could be helpful with this? I've been practicing aura viewing in particular.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content: That's a good place to start. Pay attention to changes in the aura of the neurotypical people involved, and see if you can interpret what happens to their aura in the presence of an autist.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
Hey there. Sociable and somewhat gregarious Sun in Leo here, though it is balanced by my introspective-introverted Cancer Rising. Gotta stay in my shell sometimes.

I have found what you said about my "neurotypical"-ness to be interesting. I am neurotypical... but I don't always feel typical! I do know that when I am at a concert, movie theater, or at an event with a lot of people who are emotionally charged by something, say a large family gathering, I will pick up a lot of the emotions of people, can get teary eyed or verklempt, even if I am not feeling sad per se. I don't always like it. I have for sure been honed in on by people with troubles who confide in me or need my help. Partly I think that is karma. Partly I think that is part of my work as an initiate.

I have had several people in my life who I guess are autists. I hang out here a lot too. I just wanted to say, I think maybe there is something to what you say. I thought I'd chime in from another part of the spectrum.

Neurotypical Alabaster Flummoxed Lion

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
You may be onto something there!

Wonder if there's a male/female difference in it. I don't end up with super outgoing life of the party friends generally. I do tend to attract mother-hen types among women, and end up with super-energetic, talkative, gregarious male bosses at work, with whom I got along well, but did not hang out with socially. It is a good working relationship: I appreciate people who will take on all the burden of keeping a conversation going (so I don't have to), and I think they appreciate people who do not want to share the spotlight with them. Win-win ;) In social settings, though, I find such people *exhausting*.

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content: Hiya I am a self-diagnosed ADHDer and the type of person you are describing sounds like one of us! We are often better at masking in social situations than autists. I know I am. But sometimes I get really hyper inside and its difficult to stop chatting/ laughing/ oversharing. I do find autists easier to hang out with and gravitate towards them... I always thought it was because, even before I realised what was going on with me, I just found autistic people more accepting of weird personality quirks, but perhaps there is a grounding effect... ADHD comes with a very buzzy energy hmmmm.... I'll jave to po der that!

replies.title: Re: The Emotional Grounding Effect
replies.content:
Hello and thank you for your post.
I am one of these people you describe, though I wouldn't say I'm neurotypical. I'm sometimes called an expert on autism by folks in my field. There is something refreshing and honest in personalities with autism that is attractive. It may be that it feels like i am not as likely to be taken advantage of. I would agree that it feels like I'm being grounded , or that I'm taking a break, when I'm interacting with someone that has an ASD dx.
Although I'm not sure what neurotypical is. This neurotypical vs. neurodiverse thing. I understand what the definitions are, but I disagree with the binary framework of the categories. I think my personal view is that neurotypicals don't really exist. And if they do appear to exist, perhaps this group is just acting out superficial cliches in their presenting Behavior.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good morning JMG.

I have a few questions for you today.

1) Your inclusion of Uranus and Neptune in your astrological posts leads me to conclude that you use the modern rulership scheme instead of the traditional one. On to my question, when interpreting house rulers, do you use a 'mixed' approach (eg looking at both Saturn and Uranus for a house cusp in Aquarius) or do you only look at the modern ruler? Said another way, does Jupiter and Saturn maintain some sort of influence on Pisces and Aquarius respectively in your opinion?

2) So I've been chugging along with the SOP as presented in the GSF book (but personalized to accommodate by beliefs) for a while now and I feel rather satisfied with the whole thing. However, some of the comments here sometimes mention the womb center, rising dragons, some sort of tree visualization, and whatnot in relation to the SOP. Now I assume that these things come from your more Druid-oriented books (which I don't have) so I'm completely lost when this subject gets brought up, though I can infer that these SOP variants have a different focus than the one presented in GSF. What are the differences in the long-term effects of these SOP variants? Am I missing out by sticking to what I assume is a comparatively 'simpler' SOP ritual? Also, why not include the womb center in the newer variant (I'm not challenging your decision btw, this is just my honest curiosity)?

3) I've been meditating recently on karma, the appearance of injustice, and on inflicting harm. I began asking myself if there was a karmic difference between launching a magical attack against someone versus simply walking up to them and punching them (or a more extreme example of casting a death curse vs murdering someone using mundane means). What brought this on was all the talk of the inescapable blowback of hostile magic contrasted with the grisly reality that many people in the world 'seemingly' do get away with aggrevated assault and even murder (at least in their current life). The only satisfactory way of reconciling these ideas that I've come up with is that harming someone from any level of reality will equally tip the scales of a person's character and will require an equal amount of rebalancing/compensation in the long run, however, harm inflicted from the lower levels of reality will result in a more diffuse, prolonged, and indirect karmic response whereas using magic to do the same will result in a response that is precise, swift, and to the point. What are your thoughts on this, John?

As always, thank you for hosting Magic Monday.

JR

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I use Uranus as the ruler of Aquarius and Pisces as the ruler of Neptune, full stop. I don't claim this as a universal rule, mind you -- it's just always worked for me.

2) The additional material is found in The Druid Magic Handbook and The Dolmen Arch. The Golden Section work has its own set of advanced practices that unfold from the Sphere of Protection, one of which is included toward the end of The Way of the Golden Section and the rest of which are in the subsequent books. Thus, no, you're not missing out on anything -- the GSF material is different in structure but it leads to similar effects.

3) The blowback from evil magic isn't a function of karma, though of course magical evil does generate karma that has to be worked off. The real difficulty with evil magic is that whatever energy you invoke for any purpose "sticks" to you and influences your own life. This isn't a moral issue -- it's automatic, impersonal, and inescapable, and it happens with every kind of magic (which is why doing workings for healing and blessing is such a good idea).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for these answers and for your time.

JR

I am new to the serious study, but really enjoying "Astrology of Nations."

replies.title: the old rulers in natal charts
replies.content:
Hi OP,

I have found in my natal chart that Jupiter still functions somewhat as the ruler of Pisces.

My reasoning: Mercury is in Pisces and Jupiter is in Virgo. Both planets are retrograde and in detriment and otherwise afflicted. But their influences in my chart much more beneficial than those situations would suggest...so I'm guessing they are in mutual reception, and gaining the dignity of their rulerships. For that to be the case, Jupiter would need to retain some rulership of Pisces.

--Ms. Krieger

comments.content:
Hello JMG and commenters,
Have a great start to the week, everyone!
A few random questions:
1) A friend of mine practices Magnified Healing, a type of spiritual healing treatment inspired by Master Kuan Yin. She invited me to an Initiation and I confess I don't have any information beyond what I found on websites. Are you familiar with this practice?
2) Taking advantage of the topic of healing: why do so many people speak ill of Reiki, as a malignant egregore? Is there any basis regarding the origin of the energy used in this treatment?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, I'm not. I don't tend to accept offers to receive any sort of newly invented initiation, simply because there generally hasn't been enough time to identify the downsides -- and those can be pretty considerable.

2) I think it became corrupt due to being turned into a multilevel marketing scheme -- "pay me lots of money to become a teacher, and then you can charge the same steep prices to others!" That said, I've received benefit from reiki healings, so the egregor may still be salvageable.

comments.content:
To share, for those who wonder what CosDoc, DF or TSW mean, a list of abbreviations used on Ecosophia. There's also an overview of the books discussed so far. This week is book 70 and we're in September 2022 with 3 years of books to go.

To ask, you mentioned earlier that the publisher decides on book titles and cover art. Now I wonder about the title "King in Orange". It really resonates but it doesn't have anything in the name Trump fans might find. What can you share about how this title was settled on?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The publisher has the final say on the title, but about half the time, in my experience, they accept the author's suggestion. That title was my idea, for reasons explained in the introduction.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks for your reply. I am glad they kept the title. In case others have the same question, here is the paragraph from the introduction that explains the title:

Of all the classic weird tales I read while working on The Weird of Hali, the ones that seemed to catch the flavor of our time most precisely were the linked short stories Robert W. Chambers gathered in The King in Yellow. Most of those stories are set in the ordinary world of Chambers’ time, but prosaic reality begins to twist and shudder as a different reality seeps through the crawlspaces: the reality of Carcosa, the city of the King in Yellow, where the shadows of thought lengthen in the afternoon and black stars hang in the heavens. That same vertiginous sense of shifting realities, it seemed to me, has been spreading throughout American public life in the age of Trump, and the madness that played so central and disquieting a role in Chambers’ stories was there in ample supply as well. comments.title: Mental plane exercises
comments.content:
Hi Mr. Greer,

A few weeks ago someone asked about how to develop one's mental body, and it got me thinking: would things like working math problems, logic problems, puzzles, crosswords, and similar all contribute to this? Would writing, especially analytical or critical writing? Would studying epistemology?

replies.title: Re: Mental plane exercises
replies.content:
Nope. You can do all these things on a purely astral level, so long as you're just manipulating symbols. You can get exceedingly clever at manipulating symbols and still just be playing games in the middle to upper astral plane.

To break through to the mental plane you have to go beyond the symbols into their meanings and values. That's something at which most "smart" people are no better than anyone else.

replies.content: Like this, I think - consider two different lawyers or judges. One is extremely capable of juggling and manipulating the laws - abstractions, that is - to reach a certain preordained conclusion or judgement. The other isn't as adept as the first on this sort of thing, but can pierce through the heart of the matter of a case in an instant and render fair judgements and opinions.

a) It turns out my Dreamwidth issues I mentioned over at the main blog may have been unrelated to the site. I've received an apology from my ISP over some problems they had over the last couple of days. I'm not sure why these issues affected dreamwidth and not other services, but it does look like it might be unrelated to the website if no one else has had a problem.

b) I have also been mulling over the implications of the idea of the digital age causing problems by preventing people from reaching the Great Arcanum merely by living, because it has some very interesting implications. One of them which has occurred to me is that if this is the case, then the standard method a lot of people try to use to address problem internet usage is flawed. The standard suggestion is to start with the simple stuff: minimizing social media, checking email less often, etc. This has a place, and certainly can work to reshape things in a positive fashion, but this line of reasoning suggests that another key step is to learn to do something complicated without the internet, and ideally without any electronic device. This is usually neglectated for quite some time, because it seems like it would make sense to start with the easy stuff, especially because a lot of things are genuinely a lot easier with a computer; the editing process for writing, for example, is vastly easier with a computer.

This has a hidden advantage of bringing about the extra energy and force that emerges from the Great Arcanum, and so learning to do something complicated without the internet is thus a major magical action, whether or not it is intended that way. Intriguingly, I actually have some evidence for this: I track my internet usage and have noticed that without any kind of effort on my part, it has shifted over the past few months, starting out around the same time I started to real get a sense that mathematics done by hand is different from mathematics done with a calculator.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Er, I think your basic premise is flawed. People were having just as much trouble making sense of the Great Arcanum in 1855, when typewriters were hot new technology, than they do today. For that matter, there's good reason to think that they had just as much trouble with it back when the standard writing technology involved a reed stylus and clay tablets. Mind you, I think it's a very good idea to learn to do without the internet -- not least because its longevity is by no means guaranteed -- but that has nothing to do with the Great Arcanum.

replies.content: That's a good comparison. Nobody actually flew on brooms, and there's been a lot of speculation about what was or wasn't actually going on behind the legend!

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content:
I find the brooms-as-drug trip explanation to be plausible, since it also explains why it's a social ill that people used state resources to combat (just like modern drugs) and why no serious magical texts even bother commenting on it.

The same can't be said for events like this: https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyle/penis-theft-panic-hits-city-idUSN23196036/

It seems to me that anyone who could reduce or remove a body part like the 'victims' allege could make a tidy sum without resorting to extortion - but that doesn't stop these mobs from forming on such, ahem, small accusations. I imagine events like this news story were what the low-integrity author of the Malleus Malificarum had in mind, too.

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content: Good question. It seems very, very mentally ill to me...

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content:
Maybe not necessarily mentally ill, or at least not much more than garden-variety neurosis, mixed with cultural expectations. Penile contraction and diminution can be caused by a variety of circumstances and events -- for example, persistent cold (though this is probably not so likely in Kinshasha, as opposed to northern Europe).

Anxiety, whether social anxiety, performance anxiety, or some other situational anxiety, can have such effects, and a sudden fear that one has been cursed could certainly do so -- and the effect could be self-reinforcing: noticing a small amount of shrinkage could cause more anxiety, etc., producing a nice feedback cycle. When there is already a widespread fear that this sort of curse is part of the back of tricks of malignant sorcerers, a suspicion or fear that one has been bewitched could trigger this effect in susceptible men.

Such effects are well known in non-magical contexts (see "turtling"). And curse-anxiety can lead to other kinds of dire experiences -- for example, the panics over queue-cutting, soul-stealing, sorcerers that erupted in China at various times. Note that the Manchu-imposed queue was regarded very ambivalently, and there were good reasons to be worried if one's queue had in fact been cut off. Not to mention the fear that one's soul was to be used in a foundation sacrifice. (See for example Kuhn's Soul-Stealers.)

Now, the more antic or whimsical events reported in the Malleus Maleficarum (e.g., clusters of detached male members found raising their heads in birds' nests: the hungry whatnots look up, but are not fed, so to speak) could be "explained" as misinterpretations of featherless hatchlings; finding a specimen of Phallus impudicus thrusting up from the forest floor might also lead some people to jump to conclusions.
One simply does not know. Perhaps one could get a federal grant?

LeGrand Cinq-Mars

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content: I suspect the golden age of federal grants is past. Still, thanks for this.

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content: I was going to object to that characterization of the author(s) of the Malleus Maleficarum, because when I read part of it, I had come away supposing that they could well just have been genuinely confused by various confessions and reports. But Wikipedia says historians think on the basis of side evidence that the unanimous approval from the University of Cologne theology faculty with which the book was published must have been forgery, and the book's implicit claims that Heinrich Kramer and Jacob Sprenger were collaborating on good terms must have been false, at least eventually. Maybe I'm too quick to presume good faith.

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content: In more recent times, John C. Lilly, the inventor of the sensory deprivation tank, claimed to experience this while trying to establish a telepathic link with extraterrestrials. Alien abduction stories sometimes feature it. My guess its either a reaction to locus of perception shifting to the etheric body, which has a different polarity, or some kind of etheric vampirism.

replies.title: Re: Private theft folklore?
replies.content:
It strikes me as most likely that that broadly distributed and emphatically materialized metaphor refers to the stealing, blocking, or co-opting of one's coiled kundalini energy. Since a perfectly normal way to dissipate excessive kundalini energy is to channel it sexually out through the penis or up through the uterus, any reference to stolen genitalia sounds like a theft or attempted theft or dreamed-of theft of someone's kundalini potential. Declaring a woman's womb to be barren would be a parallel curse structure.

If some creepy dark mage did figure out how to siphon off some guy's kundalini energies, further depotentizing him by declaring his penis missing would probably be considerably easier than trying to convince him that his spinal column had been magically stolen from him. Just convincing the mark that his penis was incrementally shrinking (and might eventually disappear altogether) would be enough to land a lot of guys in the loony bin. That would make for some potent magic! Or would that be impotent magic? Hard to know really... or maybe soft? Well, at least it's a shot in the dark... hopefully, that is!

— Christophe

comments.title: Re: A few GD related questions:
comments.content:
1) No, it works equally well with either center.

2) In GD magic you always banish specifically whatever you've invoked. So you would do the following working:

LBRP
LBRH
Invoking ritual of Jupiter
Incense and hymn
Keyword followed by QC to equilibrate
Thank the powers of Jupiter
Banishing ritual of Jupiter
LBRH
LBRP

3) I don't recommend that for beginners. You want to work with the elements before you go on to the planets -- plan on doing elemental workings with all four elements, scrying all the tattwas, etc. before you do the LBRH on a daily basis.

comments.title: Re: A few GD related questions:
comments.content: I recommend getting a couple of good books on GD magic, where all this is discussed in detail. You need to learn how to summon and banish each of the elements individually, and by doing so, learn how the elements affect you and what their energies are like. You also need to practice tattwa scrying, so you experience the elements in that way. I recommend one elemental working a week, in which you open a temple in the standard form, use the invocation from the grade ritual assigned to that element, meditate on the element, then use the banishing ritual and elemental prayer assigned to that element, and close the temple in due form. Do this weekly, alternating from earth to air to water to fire week by week, until you've done each element at least ten times. Then consider making some basic talismans of the elements or doing some other kind of practical magic. Again, if you're not sure what I'm talking about, close study of those GD books is called for!

replies.content: You may need to reread the book again. You draw four pentagrams, not one -- one to the east of where you're standing, one to the south, one to the west, and one to the north. All four pentagrams are upright, as though standing on their two lower points, with their one upper point aimed straight up at the ceiling.

comments.title: Imagination good wild
comments.content:
Good evening Archdruid,

When doing exercises with my imagination the objects love to spin, twist, distort, you get the idea.

Even when doing the SOP the energy going through me will swing around like those tube guys outside car dealerships.

Any idea why?

Will constant practice fix this?

replies.title: Re: Imagination good wild
replies.content: That's a problem I also had early on in my studies of magic. Yes, relentless practice will fix it.
